title: Pratyākhyāna of the Deśāvagāśika Vrata
summary: This is the short vow-formula a lay practitioner recites to take on the Deśāvagāśika, the tenth of the twelve lay vows. In it you stand before your guru and pledge, for a fixed short span of time, to shrink the world you move and act in — limiting yourself by substance, place, time, and inner attitude — so that for that period far less harm can be done to living beings. Like every Jain renunciation formula it ends by naming a few honest exceptions (an accidental slip, a sudden reflex, a greater compulsion, the need to keep one's overall calm) and then a final word of giving-up: 'vosirāmi' — 'I renounce it.'
ritualPlacement: This is not a sūtra of any of the five pratikramaṇas; it belongs to the appendix observances of the book (book pp.104–108), where the procedure for the Deśāvagāśika — the tenth lay vow — is laid out. The Deśāvagāśika vidhi has two pratyākhyānas: this one (`deshavagasik-pachchakkhan`), the renunciation-declaration proper, and `deshavagasik-samayik-pachchakkhan`, which is the Karemi Bhante sāmāyika formula adapted to the Deśāvagāśika. Per the book's sharing model, these observances do not print a fresh ritual of their own but reference the Devasi sāmāyika pages (taking and concluding a sāmāyika) with the appropriate word-substitution; they should never be described as independent pratikramaṇas.
authorAndSource: — (No individual author is named.) Like the namukkārasahiaṁ and other pratyākhyāna formulae, this is a traditional Āvaśyaka-tradition renunciation declaration in Ardhamāgadhī Prakrit; its closing 'āgāra' (exception) clauses — anābhoga, sahasā, mahattara, savva-samāhi-vattiyā — are the standard pratyākhyāna āgāras shared across the corpus. The text here follows the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) recension as printed in *Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit*.
structureArc: There is a single verse (1). It unfolds in five movements: (a) the address — 'ahannaṁ bhante! tumhāṇaṁ samīve' — standing before the guru to declare the Deśāvagāśika; (b) the four-fold scope named — davvao, khittao, kālao, bhāvao (by substance, place, time, mode); (c) each scope spelled out — by substance the Deśāvagāśika itself, by place 'here or elsewhere' (itthaṁ vā annatthaṁ vā), by time 'the measure of a muhūrta up to the measure of one's resolve' (muhuttaṁ … jāva dhāraṇā parimāṇaṁ), by mode the inner attitude maintained; (d) the abhigraha (binding resolve) held so long as one is not seized, not deceived, and no disease or change of disposition undoes it; (e) the four āgāras (permitted exceptions) and the closing renunciation 'vosirai / vosirāmi.'
recurringImagery: The governing framework is the four-fold scope — dravya (substance), kṣetra (place), kāla (time), bhāva (mode/attitude) — the same four-fold measuring stick used to bound any vow elsewhere in the corpus (cf. the `dravya-kshetra-kaal-bhav` declaration in the Devasi flow). Here it is applied to contract one's field of activity for a short, self-set span: a deliberate, temporary 'drawing-in of the circle' so that the broader directional limits of the Digvrata (the sixth vow) are tightened to a small compass for the day or muhūrta.
keyThemes: renunciation for a fixed short period (pratyākhyāna)
the tenth lay vow as a śikṣāvrata (training vow)
contracting one's field of activity to reduce harm (ahiṁsā)
the four-fold scope: substance, place, time, mode
self-discipline as preparation for stricter observance

glossary.definition: The tenth lay vow: for a fixed short period, limiting one's field of movement and activity to a restricted 'region' (deśa), tightening the broader Digvrata limits.
A formal renunciation or vow-formula by which one gives up a defined activity for a defined span of time; the sixth Āvaśyaka.
A vow or disciplined commitment undertaken by a practitioner.
A 'training vow'; the last four of the twelve lay vows, of which Deśāvagāśika is one, that school the layperson toward stricter conduct.
The sixth lay vow, fixing outer limits on travel in the directions; the Deśāvagāśika contracts these limits further for a short time.
'Substance' — the what of the vow: the objects or matters to which the restriction applies.
'Place/field' — the spatial extent within which one binds oneself ('here or elsewhere').
'Time' — the duration of the vow, here a muhūrta up to the measure of one's resolve.
'Mode/attitude' — the inner disposition with which the restriction is held.
A traditional unit of time (about 48 minutes); here it marks the minimum span of the vow.
A binding resolve or self-imposed restraint, held so long as conditions (not seized, not deceived, no disease) permit.
A 'permitted exception' named in a vow so that an unintended lapse does not break it.
Inadvertence; acting without awareness — one of the standard āgāras.
A sudden or reflexive act done without forethought — an āgāra (sahasā-gāreṇaṁ).
A greater or weightier compulsion overriding the vow — an āgāra (mahattara-gāreṇaṁ).
'For the sake of complete equanimity/well-being' — the exception allowing one to act to preserve overall calm or health.
'I renounce / I give it up' — the concluding act of relinquishment that seals a pratyākhyāna.
'I attend upon / abide in' — to dwell within the bound observance for the set period.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as 'a short promise, said to one's guru, to live within a smaller circle for a little while.' An advanced practitioner should attend to how the four-fold dravya-kṣetra-kāla-bhāva scope and the standard āgāra clauses recur identically across the corpus's pratyākhyānas, and how this vow operationally re-binds the Digvrata for a muhūrta.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)
sources.consultedFor: verbatim text and appendix placement of the Deshavagasika vidhi (book pp.104–108)

notes: The two ritual exemplar URLs aside, the source of record is the source book itself (`data/book/panch-pratikraman.full.md`); the 'sources' entry above is the print edition, not a verified stable web URL — treat the URL as a placeholder. No individual author is attested for this pratyākhyāna; it is a traditional Āvaśyaka formula. The verse shows the book's characteristic orthographic variation (e.g. `પચ્ચખ્ખાણ`, the doubled forms), preserved verbatim and not normalized. Word-division of the āgāra cluster (e.g. 'annatthaṇā bhogeṇaṁ') follows the printed text and may vary in other recensions.
